text: Hello, good morning. This is a case of laparoscopic colic isectomy and laparoscopic appendix isectomy in the same patient at the same session and we will use the same port.

The surgeon begins the cholecystectomy procedure, using a harmonic scalpel visible below the Rouviere's sulcus. The appendix is noted to be retrocecal and subserous, while the gallbladder appears uncomplicated.

Using the harmonic scalpel, the surgeon carefully opens the posterior peritoneal leaf of the gallbladder. The Teflon and silicon jaws of the harmonic are kept oriented towards the gallbladder to prevent rupture, as the peritoneum is gradually opened at the angle of the cystic pedicle and the body of the gallbladder.

The surgeon applies entrolateral traction using the harmonic device, positioning its silicon jaw towards the liver for slight retraction. The tip of the harmonic is used to carefully open the peritoneum without puncturing any vessels.
The surgeon strips the peritoneum over the cystic pedicle and gradually opens the entire peritoneum surrounding it using a harmonic device during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to perform further skeletonization, creating a window by opening the small jaws and gradually enlarging them to approximately 22 millimeters. The inferior surface of the liver becomes visible as the surrounding adventitious tissue is dissected. The cystic duct, noted to be very thin, is skeletonized.

The surgeon identifies the Calot's triangle, revealing the cystic duct and artery behind it, along with the inferior surface of the liver. The cystic duct is prepared for ligation using an extracorporeal knot, specifically Mishra's knot.

The surgeon uses a 90 cm number one bike rail to tie a secure extracorporeal knot around the cystic duct, employing a series of hitches, winds, and locks to form a dumbbell-shaped knot. A knot pusher is used to tighten the knot, ensuring it is secure up to a 22 mm diameter. The harmonic device is used to coagulate the cystic duct and artery, with traction released to facilitate separation.

The surgeon applies entromedial and entrolateral traction to cut the peritoneal soldier and separate the body of the gallbladder from the liver, maintaining a clear areolar plane to minimize bleeding and prevent accidental rupture. The gallbladder is now half-separated, and the 30-degree telescope light cable is adjusted to improve visibility of the fossa. The gallbladder is further separated from the liver, ensuring a good areolar plane throughout the process.

The surgeon uses a cloth forceps to crush and remove a 22-millimeter stone from the gallbladder. The telescope is transferred to the epigastric port, while graspers are positioned in the umbilical port for better visualization and manipulation.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector through the right hypochondriac port to perform dissection around the base of a deeply retrocecal appendix. The epigastric port holds the telescope, providing a view of the separated epiploic appendages, while the tip of the appendix remains obscured.

The surgeon uses blunt dissection and harmonic energy sources to carefully separate the deep retrocecal appendix, progressing towards its tip. The appendix is gradually lifted and separated, requiring an anti-grade approach due to its length and depth.

The surgeon adjusts the traction on the appendix, moving the graspers closer to the tip. Careful application of traction is necessary to avoid breaking the appendix. The tip of the appendix becomes slightly visible, allowing for a combination of blunt and electrosurgical dissection.

The surgeon dissects the appendix, which is partially free at the tip but mostly attached to surrounding fat. The appendix is completely separated using skeletonization to reach the base, ensuring no incomplete appendectomy. A pre-tied loop with number one vicryl is applied at the base, and the knot is secured using scissors. The harmonic device is used to seal and burn the mucosa. The appendix is placed in an endo bag through the ten millimeter umbilical port and removed. The telescope is positioned in the epigastric region, and the port is closed using a variational technique with a suture loop.
